alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed bioengineering and machine learning framework optimizes organoid symmetry breaking by tuning their spatial coupling. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a novel contribution about a bioengineering and machine-learning framework for tuning organoid symmetry breaking that does not appear in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study identifies 84 groups of independently modulated genes (iModulons) that explain 75.7% of the total variance in the gene expression compendium. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study identifies 84 groups of independently modulated genes (iModulons) that explain 75.7% of the total variance in the gene expression compendium. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication texts are identical in content and metrics, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research expands the understanding of the regulatory functions of 39 iModulon associated transcription factors by comparing them with previously reported TF-gene interactions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research expands the understanding of the regulatory functions of 39 iModulon associated transcription factors by comparing them with previously reported TF-gene interactions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Wording and scope are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study outlines transcriptional changes that occur after the transition from exponential growth to stationary phases in Pseudomonas putida. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study outlines transcriptional changes that occur after the transition from exponential growth to stationary phases in Pseudomonas putida KT2440. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds the specific strain (KT2440), which clarifies and narrows the taxonomic scope without changing the substance of the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research captures a group of genes required for utilizing diverse carbon sources and increased stationary response with slower growth rates. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research captures a group of genes required for utilizing diverse carbon sources and increased stationary response with slower growth rates. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text and meaning are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides the first quantitative genome-scale transcriptional regulatory network for Pseudomonas putida. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides the first quantitative genome-scale transcriptional regulatory network for Pseudomonas putida KT2440. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication specifies the strain (KT2440), narrowing the scope while preserving the original contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ImmuNet consistently outperforms a state-of-the-art segmentation-based pipeline for multiplex immunohistochemistry analysis across tissue types, cell types, and tissue densities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ImmuNet outperforms a segmentation-based baseline method in identifying cell positions and phenotypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims state ImmuNet outperforms segmentation-based methods, but the publication frames the improvement specifically for identifying positions and phenotypes and omits the explicit across-tissues phrasing, narrowing/clarifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ImmuNet achieves error rates below 5-10% on challenging detection and phenotyping tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ImmuNet consistently achieves error rates below 5%-10% across various tissue types, cell types, and tissue densities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric error-rate claim and scope across tissue/cell types match between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ImmuNet is an effective, simpler alternative to segmentation-based approaches when only cell positions and phenotypes, but not their shapes, are required for downstream analyses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ImmuNet provides a simpler alternative to segmentation-based approaches for analyzing cell positions in multiplex tissue images. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication emphasizes cell-position analysis and omits explicit mention of phenotypes and excluding shapes, which narrows and clarifies the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ImmuNet allows human annotators to annotate cells by simply clicking on an immune cell and scoring its expression of each marker. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human annotators only need to click on an immune cell and score its expression of each marker to train ImmuNet. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same contribution regarding the annotation protocol required to train/use ImmuNet.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The choice of parcellation significantly influences out-of-sample predictive performance in neuroimaging studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The choice of parcellation significantly influences out-of-sample predictive performance in neuroimaging studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical conclusion in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Larger numbers of parcels, up to approximately 4000, outperform coarser parcellations according to a power-law scaling of between 1/4 and 1/3. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Larger parcellations, with up to ~4,000 parcels, outperform coarser parcellations according to a power-law scaling of between 1/4 and 1/3. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same quantitative finding and scaling relationship in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The type of parcellation, machine learning pipeline, and general strategy all affect predictive performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The type of parcellation, machine learning pipeline, and general strategy further influence predictive performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same factors and conclusion are stated in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Switching to a higher resolution parcellation can yield a relatively large boost to predictive performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Switching to a higher resolution parcellation can yield a relatively large boost to predictive performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim is verbatim and unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interpretable machine learning platform can rigorously assess hundreds of candidate mechanisms per disease. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TRACE, a machine learning approach, predicts genes that underlie tissue-selective diseases and selectivity-related features. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Preprint emphasizes assessing many candidate mechanisms per disease; the publication reframes the contribution around predicting genes and features (a broader, differently phrased capability), so the claim is revised and broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Application of the platform to selected Mendelian disease genes pinpointed mechanisms that lead to tissue-specific disease manifestation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Application of TRACE to 1,031 disease genes uncovered known and novel selectivity-related features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports application at a much larger scale (1,031 genes) and frames results as uncovering selectivity-related features rather than pinpointing mechanisms for a selected set, so the result is revised and broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patient-specific models of candidate disease-causing genes from 50 patients successfully prioritized the pathogenic gene in 86% of the cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TRACE ranked the verified disease gene among the patient's candidate genes significantly better than gene prioritization methods that rank by gene constraint or tissue expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint gives a specific 86% prioritization rate for 50 patients; the publication instead reports a comparative ranking improvement without the 86% figure, so the claim was revised, narrowed in explicit metric, and the confidence framing is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tissue-selectivity of rare diseases aids in filtering out unlikely candidate genes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This specific conclusion about using tissue-selectivity to filter candidates does not appear in the publication claims and is therefore treated as removed.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The most common selectivity-related feature uncovered by TRACE was previously overlooked. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a claim about a previously overlooked common selectivity-related feature that has no counterpart in the preprint claims, so it is added.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed model classifies metals bound to proteins as enzymatic or non-enzymatic with 92.2% precision and 90.1% recall. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed model classifies metals bound to proteins as enzymatic or non-enzymatic with 92.2% precision and 90.1% recall. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match of reported performance metrics and claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Electrostatic and pocket lining features are scored as more important than pocket volume in distinguishing enzymatic from non-enzymatic sites. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Electrostatic and pocket lining features are scored as more important than pocket volume in distinguishing enzymatic from non-enzymatic sites. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim about feature importance is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrates better performance in differentiating enzymatic from non-enzymatic sequences compared to other methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrates better performance in differentiating enzymatic from non-enzymatic sequences compared to other methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance comparison claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ability of the model to identify metal sites responsible for enzymatic activity could enable the identification of new enzymatic mechanisms and de novo enzyme design. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ability of the model to identify metal sites responsible for enzymatic activity could enable the identification of new enzymatic mechanisms and de novo enzyme design. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Concluding implication about enabling discovery and design is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PeptideForest increases the number of peptide-to-spectrum matches that exhibit a q-value lower than 1% by 25.2 ± 1.6% compared to MS-GF+ data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PeptideForest increases the number of peptide-to-spectrum matches that exhibit a q-value lower than 1% by 25.2 ± 1.6% compared to MS-GF+ data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and reported effect are effectively identical between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The increase in peptide-to-spectrum matches below 1% q-value does not come with a decrease in quantification quality. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The increase in peptide-to-spectrum matches below 1% q-value does not come with a decrease in quantification quality. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is stated identically in the publication, with no added or removed qualifiers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PeptideForest offers a possibility to gain deeper insights into bottom-up proteomics. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PeptideForest offers a possibility to gain deeper insights into bottom-up proteomics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The substantive claim is the same; only the label (contribution vs conclusion) differs, not the claim content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PeptideForest integrates the assignments of multiple algorithms to train a random forest classifier. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PeptideForest has been integrated into the pipeline framework Ursgal and can be combined with a wide array of algorithms. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the implementation detail: instead of focusing on RF training from multiple algorithm assignments, it emphasizes pipeline integration and broader algorithm compatibility, a substantive change in focus.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spatiotemporal variability during gait is linked to fall risk and can be monitored using wearable sensors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spatiotemporal variability during gait is linked to fall risk and can be monitored using wearable sensors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim appears verbatim in the publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: xGB models were best for single-stride outcomes with a relative mean absolute error of 7–11% and an intraclass correlation coefficient of 0.60–0.86. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: xGB models were found to be the best for predicting single-stride outcomes with a relative mean absolute error of 7–11%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the key MAE performance claim but omits ICC values from the preprint, narrowing the reported metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SVM models were best for spatiotemporal variability with a mean absolute error of 18–22% and an intraclass correlation coefficient of 0.47–0.64. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SVM models were determined to be the best for assessing spatiotemporal variability with a mean absolute error of 18–22%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication keeps the MAE performance but drops ICC reporting, so the finding is preserved but with narrower reported evidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spatiotemporal changes with speed were captured by the models, with a significant condition effect (p < 0.00625). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models successfully captured spatiotemporal changes with speed, as indicated by a significant p-value (p < 0.00625). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result and statistical support are preserved with comparable wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results support the feasibility of monitoring multi-stride spatiotemporal parameters using a smartwatch IMU and machine learning. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study supports the feasibility of using a smartwatch IMU and machine learning to monitor spatiotemporal parameters. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated in the publication with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean-field formalism derived encompasses synaptic shot-noise for sparse balanced networks of spiking neurons. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The specific technical claim about encompassing synaptic shot-noise does not appear in the publication's listed claims and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Irregular global oscillations emerge via continuous and hysteretic transitions for low and high external drives and synaptic strengths. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Global oscillations emerge via continuous or hysteretic transitions under low excitatory drive and high inhibitory feedback, as predicted by our mean-field formalism. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core result about continuous/hysteretic transitions but specifies conditions (low excitatory drive, high inhibitory feedback), clarifying the original phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach correctly predicts the emergence of oscillations, which is not achieved by the diffusion approximation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean-field formalism correctly predicts global oscillations, which cannot be accurately described by the diffusion approximation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same conclusion contrasting the mean-field formalism with the diffusion approximation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The oscillations display frequencies in biologically relevant bands. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not include mention of oscillation frequencies in biologically relevant bands, so this result appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: At sufficiently low in-degrees, the nature of global oscillations changes from drift driven to cluster activation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This claim about in-degree-dependent changes is present in the publication but has no counterpart in the preprint, representing an added result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Analytic continuation of results for slightly deformed Lorentzian distributions is impossible due to the divergence of all the moments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analytic continuation of results for slightly deformed Lorentzian distributions is impossible due to the divergence of all the moments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is restated verbatim in the publication, with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A pseudo-cumulants’ expansion has been introduced to solve the problem of analytic continuation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A 'pseudocumulants' expansion has been introduced to solve the problem of analytic continuation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is described the same way in the publication (minor orthographic difference only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pseudo-cumulants’ expansion allows for the development of a reduction methodology for heterogeneous spiking neural networks. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed reduction methodology allows for a unified mean-field formulation for heterogeneous spiking neural networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes explicit that the reduction methodology yields a unified mean-field formulation, broadening and clarifying the practical outcome of the pseudo-cumulants expansion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The unified mean-field formulation developed encompasses both quenched and dynamical disorder sources. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The unified mean-field formulation encompasses both quenched and dynamical sources of disorder. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication presents it with higher stated certainty, indicating amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three neural networks contribute to Anosognosia for Hemiplegia when disconnected: the premotor loop, limbic system, and ventral attention network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Three neural systems contribute to Anosognosia for Hemiplegia when disconnected or directly damaged: the premotor loop, limbic system, and ventral attentional network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the conditions (adding 'directly damaged') under which the three systems contribute, changing the claim's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Human motor awareness is contingent on the joint contribution of the premotor loop, limbic system, and ventral attention network. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human motor awareness is contingent on the joint contribution of the premotor loop, limbic system, and ventral attentional network. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prior studies have predominantly explored discreet lesions in the context of Anosognosia for Hemiplegia. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prior studies have predominantly explored discreet lesions in the context of Anosognosia for Hemiplegia. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation statement is the same, but the publication reports lower certainty, indicating a tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The baseline chromosomal abnormality (CA) rate approximately doubles in TP53-deficient cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The baseline chromosomal abnormality (CA) mutation rate approximately doubles in TP53-deficient cells.
alignments.rationale: Wording and meaning are effectively identical; publication simply adds the word 'mutation' without altering the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Chromosome losses arise more rapidly than gains during CA formation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Chromosome losses arise more frequently than gains during CA formation.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with minor wording differences (rapidly vs frequently) that do not change the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The targeted induction of DNA double-strand breaks along chromosomes triggers distinct CA processes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The targeted induction of DNA double-strand breaks along chromosome arms triggers distinct CA processes.
alignments.rationale: Publication specifies 'chromosome arms', a small clarification of the locus of breaks while preserving the original finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MAGIC provides insights into de novo CA formation, paving the way to unravel fundamental determinants of chromosome instability.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MAGIC advances the dissection of DNA rearrangement processes and sheds light on fundamental determinants of chromosomal instability.
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the contribution more strongly and with higher certainty (phrasing is more assertive), so the claim is clarified and the confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed intrafamilial successor prediction model predicts the relative abundance of each bacterial family using the past four generations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed intrafamilial model predicts the relative abundance of bacterial families within themselves using their past generations.
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the same model claim but drops the explicit 'four generations' detail, making the description slightly more general.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interfamilial quantitative prediction model predicts the amount of a given bacterial family in each sample using the amount of all other bacteria present in the sample.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quantitative model predicts the number of bacterial families in a given sample based on the presence of other families in that sample.
alignments.rationale: The publication's wording shifts to predicting 'number of bacterial families' and uses presence rather than amounts, which changes the quantitative nature of the preprint claim; mapping is plausible but uncertain.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interfamilial qualitative prediction model predicts the relative abundance of each bacterial family within a sample using binary information of all bacterial families.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The qualitative model predicts the relative abundance using binary information of all bacterial families.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same qualitative model claim with essentially identical meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The third approach, using Random Forest, exhibits promising results with the lowest mean Coefficient of Variance of 1.25.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The third approach exhibits promising results by applying a random forest with the lowest mean coefficient of variance of 1.25.
alignments.rationale: The result is reproduced verbatim in the publication; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall results of this study demonstrate that more computationally efficient methods can characterise complex dynamic systems quickly.
alignments.rationale: No corresponding conclusion about computational efficiency appears in the publication; the publication emphasizes applicative outcomes instead.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicting microbiome composition can provide important data for creating more efficient and long-lasting pest control methods.
alignments.rationale: This applied pest-control implication appears in the publication but has no counterpart in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) can be a scalable, label-free method for detection of bacteria in wastewater.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) can be a scalable, label-free method for the detection of bacteria in wastewater.
alignments.rationale: Claim is effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transmission cryoelectron microscopy (cryoEM) confirms that AuNRs bind selectively to bacteria in the wastewater matrix.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The binding of plasmonic gold nanorods (AuNRs) to the bacterial surface is confirmed using cryoelectron microscopy.
alignments.rationale: Publication confirms AuNR binding via cryoEM but omits explicit 'selectively' and 'wastewater matrix' language, narrowing the contextual claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For bacterial concentrations of 10^9 cells/mL, we achieve an accuracy exceeding 85% using a machine learning model to identify bacterial species in wastewater.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model trained on 540 spectra can identify four clinically relevant bacterial species with more than 87% accuracy.
alignments.rationale: Publication gives a more specific dataset and target (540 spectra, four species) and reports a slightly higher accuracy, changing and narrowing the original, more general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system is effective at environmentally-realistic bacterial concentrations, with a limit of bacterial detection of 10^4 cells/mL.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The environmentally realistic limit of detection for the method is 10<sup>4</sup> cells/mL.
alignments.rationale: The limit-of-detection claim is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models can classify dinosaur footprints as either ornithopods or theropods with good accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models can classify dinosaur footprints as either ornithopods or theropods with good accuracy.
alignments.rationale: Claims are effectively identical in wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The recall of ornithopods is generally lower than that of theropods due to fewer samples in the data set.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The recall of ornithopods is generally lower than that of theropods due to fewer samples in the data set.
alignments.rationale: Publication reproduces the same observation and explanation about recall differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Variable selection using logistic forward regression identifies landmarks that are intuitively expected to be informative for footprint classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Variable selection using logistic forward regression identifies informative landmark locations on footprints.
alignments.rationale: Publication tightens wording to 'informative landmark locations' but preserves the original meaning that variable selection finds expected informative landmarks.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model is the most effective at handling class imbalance in footprint classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) model is the most effective at addressing class imbalance in footprint classification.
alignments.rationale: The result is restated with synonymous wording and no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Some misclassified footprints exhibit features characteristic of the other class or have compromised shapes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Some misclassified footprints exhibit features characteristic of the other class or have compromised shapes.
alignments.rationale: Identical observation about misclassified footprints present in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gray matter and white matter networks associated with mindfulness include structures such as the cingulate, insula, and basal ganglia.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Specific GM and WM structures are involved in mindfulness and different forms of mind wandering.
alignments.rationale: Publication conveys the same finding in broader terms; the preprint lists specific structures while the paper summarizes that specific GM/WM structures are involved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mindfulness exerts direct effects on both deliberate and spontaneous mind wandering.
alignments.pub_claim_text: One GM network involving subcortical regions positively predicted mindfulness and deliberate mind wandering while negatively influencing spontaneous mind wandering.
alignments.rationale: Publication specifies differential directions of effect (positive for deliberate, negative for spontaneous) mediated by a GM network, which refines the preprint's simpler statement of 'direct effects' on both forms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mindfulness facet acting with awareness has an indirect mediating effect on spontaneous mind wandering through a significant brain network.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 'acting with awareness' facet has a mediating effect on spontaneous mind wandering.
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the mediating-role claim for the 'acting with awareness' facet, consistent with the preprint's statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study expands our knowledge on the neural bases of mindfulness and mind wandering.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings have implications for ongoing research in mindfulness-based interventions and other clinical applications.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the general conclusion into specific implications for research and clinical applications and presents it with more moderate certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DrivR-Base efficiently extracts and integrates molecular information for single nucleotide variants from various databases and tools.
alignments.pub_claim_text: DrivR-Base efficiently extracts and integrates molecular information related to single nucleotide variants.
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is effectively the same as the preprint claim in scope and content, restating efficient extraction and integration of SNV-related molecular information.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The features generated by DrivR-Base can be used as input for machine learning models to predict the pathogenic impact of human genome variants in disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The resulting features from DrivR-Base can be used as input for machine learning models to predict the pathogenic impact of human genome variants in disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim about using extracted features as ML inputs to predict pathogenic impacts with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The feature sets from DrivR-Base have applications in haploinsufficiency prediction and the development of drug repurposing tools.
alignments.pub_claim_text: DrivR-Base has applications beyond pathogenic impact prediction, including haploinsufficiency prediction and drug repurposing tool development.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same specific applications but frames them as part of broader utility ("beyond pathogenic impact prediction"), clarifying context without changing scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of 288 scRNA-seq analysis pipelines to 86 datasets demonstrates that prediction performance is significantly better than random.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction performance of the models is significantly better than random.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report that model prediction performance exceeds random; the publication frames it concisely but preserves the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pipelines predicted to perform well provide clustering outputs similar to expert-annotated cell type labels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pipelines predicted to perform well often yield clustering outputs similar to expert-annotated cell type labels.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same claim but softens frequency language (adds "often"), reducing certainty compared with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Characteristics of scRNA-seq datasets correlate with strong prediction performance, which could guide the utility of prediction models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Certain characteristics of datasets correlate with strong prediction performance, which can guide the use of prediction models.
alignments.rationale: This is a restatement of the same conclusion about dataset characteristics correlating with prediction performance and guiding model use.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combined approach allowed for precise measurement of the duration of different cell cycle phases in non-adherent cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed method enables precise measurement of cell cycle phase durations in non-adherent cells.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core result about precise measurement of cell cycle phase durations in non-adherent cells without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method provided detailed information from hundreds of cells under different experimental conditions in a fully automated manner.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tools developed provide a fast, easy, and cost-effective method for studying the cell cycle.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes general benefits (fast, easy, cost-effective) and omits the specific claim about hundreds of cells and varied conditions; this is a revision that shifts emphasis away from the original detailed throughput claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cell cycle analysis system has been validated for use with adherent cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The cell cycle analysis system is fully automated and compatible with both adherent and non-adherent cells.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the claim to explicitly state compatibility with both cell types (broadening applicability) while keeping the validation claim intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This report introduces a comprehensive, automated method for studying the cell cycle in both adherent and non-adherent cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The cell cycle analysis system is fully automated and compatible with both adherent and non-adherent cells.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication assert a comprehensive, fully automated method compatible with adherent and non-adherent cells; wording is equivalent in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LIgO is a software suite that simulates AIRR data for the development and benchmarking of AIRR-ML methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LIgO is a software suite that simulates AIRR data for the development and benchmarking of AIRR-ML methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the contribution claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LIgO incorporates different types of immune information and preserves native-like generation probability distribution.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LIgO incorporates different types of immune information and preserves native-like generation probability distribution.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same capabilities and properties of LIgO.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LIgO supports the development and validation of AIRR-ML methods by demonstrating the impact of out-of-distribution immune information on receptor-level prediction performance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LIgO supports the development and validation of AIRR-ML methods by demonstrating the impact of out-of-distribution immune information on receptor-level prediction performance.
alignments.rationale: The result about demonstrating the impact of out-of-distribution information is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LIgO shows how immune information co-occurring in the same AIRs affects the performance of conventional receptor-level encoding and repertoire-level classification approaches.
alignments.pub_claim_text: LIgO shows how immune information co-occurring in the same AIRs impacts the performance of conventional receptor-level encoding and repertoire-level classification approaches.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding about co-occurring immune information and its impact on method performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final model achieved an overall 1.36 °C root-mean-square error during model testing.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final model achieved an overall 1.36°C root-mean-square error during model testing.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication state the identical RMSE result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final model outperformed two previous models using three test cultivars at all testing regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final model outperformed two previous models using three test cultivars at all testing regions.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the performance-comparison claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model might have adequately extracted some biological mechanisms during training.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model has adequately extracted some biological mechanisms during training.
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the language from tentative ("might have") to an affirmative statement ("has"), increasing confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final model, named NYUS.2, enables large-scale and real-time simulation of grapevine freezing tolerance in North America for the first time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final model, named NYUS.2, enables large-scale and real-time simulation of grapevine freezing tolerance in North America for the first time.
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim about NYUS.2 enabling large-scale real-time simulation is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeCOIL is a generalized method that directly optimizes degenerate codon libraries for protein engineering. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeCOIL is a generalized method that directly optimizes degenerate codon libraries for protein engineering. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is effectively identical in wording and meaning to the preprint contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeCOIL effectively samples protein variants that are likely to have both high fitness and high diversity in the sequence search space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeCOIL effectively samples protein variants that are likely to have both high fitness and high diversity in the sequence search space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result claim is restated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeCOIL offers several advantages over existing methods, including being direct, easy-to-use, generalizable, and scalable. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeCOIL offers several advantages over existing methods, including being direct, easy to use, generalizable, and scalable. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative advantages with only minor stylistic edits.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational simulations and wet-lab experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of DeCOIL across two specific case studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include an explicit claim that the two case studies demonstrate effectiveness; instead it adds a broader application statement, so the specific demonstration claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Antagonistic and mutualistic networks can be distinguished by combining a meso-scale characterization of their structure and supervised machine learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Antagonistic and mutualistic networks can be distinguished by combining a meso-scale characterization of their structure and supervised machine learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same methodological and result claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Motif frequencies are particularly informative, with an over-representation of densely connected motifs in antagonistic networks and of motifs with asymmetrical specialization in mutualistic networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Motif frequencies are particularly informative, showing an over-representation of densely connected motifs in antagonistic networks and motifs with asymmetrical specialization in mutualistic networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same finding about motif frequency patterns.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The characteristics of network structure can be used to predict interaction types with relatively good confidence. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The structural properties of ecological networks can be used to predict the type of interaction with relatively good confidence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the predictive-value claim with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is significant structural uniqueness linked to specific ecologies, such as pollination and parasitism. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is significant structural uniqueness linked to specific ecologies, such as pollination and parasitism. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this observation about ecology-linked structural uniqueness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The investigation of the structural uniqueness of specific ecologies is a promising approach for characterizing interactions beyond the coarse antagonistic/mutualistic dichotomy. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The investigation of the structural uniqueness of specific ecologies is a promising approach for characterizing interactions beyond the antagonistic/mutualistic dichotomy. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about investigating structural uniqueness is carried over with equivalent emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RENAISSANCE accurately characterizes unknown intracellular metabolic states, including metabolic fluxes and metabolite concentrations in E. coli's metabolic network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RENAISSANCE accurately characterizes intracellular metabolic states in Escherichia coli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core result about accurately characterizing E. coli metabolic states with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RENAISSANCE successfully estimates missing kinetic parameters and reconciles them with sparse and noisy experimental data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RENAISSANCE estimates missing kinetic parameters and reconciles them with sparse experimental data, substantially reducing parameter uncertainty and improving accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the preprint claim by explicitly reporting the downstream benefits (reduced uncertainty and improved accuracy), strengthening the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework results in a substantial reduction in parameter uncertainty and a notable improvement in the accuracy and reliability of the parameter estimates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RENAISSANCE estimates missing kinetic parameters and reconciles them with sparse experimental data, substantially reducing parameter uncertainty and improving accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim directly matches the preprint's stated outcomes about uncertainty reduction and accuracy improvement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The challenges associated with determining the kinetic parameters that underlie cellular physiology pose significant obstacles to the broader acceptance and adoption of kinetic models within the research community. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this explicit critical statement about community adoption barriers; instead it focuses on the framework's technical contributions and utility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NS-Forest version 2.0 algorithm effectively discovers minimal marker gene expression combinations that capture cell type identity from scRNA-seq transcriptional profiles. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The NS-Forest version 2.0 algorithm effectively discovers minimal marker gene expression combinations that optimally capture cell type identity in scRNA-seq profiles. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core algorithmic result with only minor wording changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The selected marker genes provide a barcode of necessary and sufficient characteristics for semantic cell type definition. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The marker genes selected by NS-Forest provide an expression barcode that is a useful tool for downstream biological investigation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens the strong 'necessary and sufficient' framing to a more modest 'useful tool' for downstream work, narrowing and tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using NS-Forest to identify marker genes for human brain middle temporal gyrus cell types highlights the significance of cell signaling and non-coding RNAs in neuronal cell type identity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of NS-Forest to human brain middle temporal gyrus cell types highlights the significance of cell signaling and noncoding RNAs in defining neuronal cell type identity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same empirical finding but presents it with stronger certainty and slightly clarified wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An age-related 'neural shift' is associated with increased sleep dysfunction and decreased fluid intelligence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This 'neural shift' was associated with both increased sleep dysfunction and decreased fluid intelligence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly reports the same association with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The occurrence of 'lower-order' brain networks decreases while 'higher-order' networks increase with age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An age-related 'neural shift' is expressed as decreased occurrence of 'lower-order' brain networks coupled with increased occurrence of 'higher-order' networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this age-related shift in network occurrences directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Poor sleep quality in aging may lead to a behavior-related shift in neural dynamics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The relationship between poor sleep quality and a behavior-related shift in neural dynamics was not explained by age, sex or other covariates. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens and clarifies the original conclusion by showing the relationship persists after controlling for covariates, increasing confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Transfer occurs during unsupervised learning, but the extent and nature of this transfer depend on whether the knowledge is explicit or implicit. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Transfer occurs during unsupervised learning, but with important differences depending on the explicitness or implicitness of the acquired knowledge. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent meaning and certainty about the role of explicitness versus implicitness in transfer.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Observers acquiring explicit knowledge during initial learning can transfer the learned structures immediately. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Observers acquiring explicit knowledge during initial learning could transfer the learned structures immediately. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim is effectively identical in content and certainty to the preprint claim about immediate transfer with explicit knowledge.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Observers with implicit knowledge experience structural interference during transfer, unlike those with explicit knowledge. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Implicit observers, while still remaining implicit, switched their behavior after sleep and showed the same pattern of transfer as explicit observers did. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert generalized structural interference for implicit observers; instead it emphasizes a sleep-related change, revising the original claim and presenting it with lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After sleep, implicit observers changed their behavior to exhibit transfer patterns similar to those of explicit observers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Implicit observers, while still remaining implicit, switched their behavior after sleep and showed the same pattern of transfer as explicit observers did. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the sleep-related behavioral switch but adds the detail that observers 'remain implicit' and reports this with lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effect of sleep on transfer is specific and not observed after non-sleep consolidation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The effect of sleep on transfer learning was specific to sleep and not found after non-sleep consolidation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same specific claim about sleep versus non-sleep consolidation with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Improved accuracy for previously learned high gain relative to low gain cues emerged with age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Improved accuracy for previously learned high gain relative to low gain cues emerged with age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim matches the preprint claim verbatim in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Older adolescents exhibited enhanced recruitment of the dorsal striatum and ventrolateral prefrontal cortex during cognitive control execution to previously learned high gain relative to low gain cues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Older adolescents exhibited enhanced recruitment of the dorsal striatum and ventrolateral prefrontal cortex during cognitive control execution to previously learned high gain relative to low gain cues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical neuroimaging finding presented with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Older adolescents expressed increased coupling between the dorsal striatum and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex for high gain cues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Older adolescents expressed increased coupling between the dorsal striatum and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex for high gain cues. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same specific connectivity result with equal certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Younger adolescents expressed increased coupling between the striatum and ventromedial prefrontal cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Younger adolescents expressed increased coupling between the striatum and ventromedial prefrontal cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The connectivity pattern for younger adolescents is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Learned high value cue-incentive associations enhance cognitive control in late adolescence in parallel with value-selective recruitment of corticostriatal systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Learned high value cue-incentive associations enhance cognitive control in late adolescence in parallel with value-selective recruitment of corticostriatal systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding interpretation is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Decisions in loss-contexts are slower, supporting the Pavlovian-instrumental transfer (PIT) hypothesis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Decisions in loss-contexts are slower than in gain-contexts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same robust RT slowing in loss contexts with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Loss contexts decrease individuals’ confidence in their choices, indicating a bias similar to a Pavlovian-to-metacognitive transfer (PMT). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Loss contexts decrease individuals’ confidence in their choices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the confidence decrement result but omits the explicit PMT framing, clarifying the empirical finding while keeping certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both PIT and PMT effects were observed despite attempts to disrupt the PIT effects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Valence-induced motor and confidence biases are robust and replicable. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the persistence of both motor (PIT) and confidence (PMT) biases as robustness/replicability, which clarifies and generalizes the preprint's experimental resilience claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The confidence bias resists the disruption of the reaction time bias, suggesting that PMT and PIT are partly dissociable. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The confidence bias resists the disruption of the reaction time bias. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core empirical observation about the confidence bias resisting disruption is retained identically and with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results highlight new mechanistic constraints that should be incorporated in learning models to explain choice, reaction times, and confidence. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include the preprint's explicit recommendation about updating mechanistic learning models, so this contribution is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study found that more brain regions showed activity patterns consistent with the consistency account of similarity representation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Activity in brain areas linked to reward and social cognition followed the consistency account. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the types of brain areas (reward and social cognition) rather than the broader claim of 'more regions,' narrowing and clarifying the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural activation was modeled using reinforcement learning models based on the similarity and consistency accounts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study combined functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) and computational modelling of reinforcement learning (RL) to investigate the neural processes that underlie learning about preference similarity. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication describe the use of RL computational modeling with fMRI; the published description is slightly less certain about contribution, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest that impressions of other people are calculated in a person-specific manner, assuming consistency with past choices. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings suggest that impressions of other people can be calculated in a person-specific manner. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The central conclusion about person-specific impression formation remains but the publication drops the explicit 'assuming consistency' phrasing and reports the conclusion with lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dopamine medication in Parkinson's disease reduces an overemphasis on negative outcomes during learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dopaminergic medication reduces an overemphasis on negative outcomes during learning in Parkinson's disease patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about dopaminergic medication reducing negative-outcome overemphasis with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When on medication, Parkinson's disease patients exhibit lower learning rates for negative outcomes compared to positive outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Medication reduces negative (but not positive) outcome learning rates in Parkinson's disease patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim conveys the same asymmetry in learning rates induced by medication with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Medication-induced shifts in negative learning rates are predictive of changes in approach/avoidance choice patterns after learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Medication-induced shifts in negative learning rates are predictive of changes in approach/avoidance choice patterns after learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The predictive relationship between learning-rate shifts and subsequent approach/avoidance behavior is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Alterations in striatal BOLD responses accompany changes in approach/avoidance patterns in Parkinson's disease patients on dopaminergic medication. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Changes in learning due to dopaminergic medication impact subsequent value-based decision-making in Parkinson's disease patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the behavioral/decision-making impact of medication rather than explicitly reporting the striatal BOLD accompaniment, so the neural claim is reframed into a broader behavioral conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Corrective feedback on perceptual decisions is crucial for adjusting decision-making strategies to improve future choices. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Corrective feedback received on perceptual decisions is crucial for adjusting decision-making strategies to improve future choices. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The two claims are the same claim verbatim except the publication reports lower certainty; content and scope are unchanged but confidence is reduced.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 'feedback-as-evidence' hypothesis outperforms the 'feedback-as-reward' hypothesis in explaining how corrective feedback adjusts decision-making strategies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 'feedback-as-evidence' hypothesis outperforms the 'feedback-as-reward' hypothesis in explaining how corrective feedback adjusts decision-making strategies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is stated identically in preprint and publication with the same confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Humans learn about what has happened in their environment rather than the values of their own choices through corrective feedback during perceptual decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Humans consider corrective feedback on perceptual decisions as evidence of the actual state of the world rather than as rewards for their choices. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication rephrases the preprint conclusion more directly (feedback as evidence vs. feedback-as-reward), increasing asserted confidence; core meaning is the same but wording and certainty are clarified/amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Asymmetric scaling captures reward prediction error coding diversity in human dorsomedial prefrontal cortex and insula. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a bias towards positive reward prediction errors (RPEs) in both the insula and dorsomedial prefrontal cortex, which parallels behavioral adaptation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's broad claim about asymmetric scaling capturing coding diversity is restated in the publication as a specific observable bias toward positive RPEs; scope is narrowed to a particular empirical pattern.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural populations in dMPFC and INS respond to valence-specific positive and negative reward prediction errors, as well as unsigned reward prediction error salience. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spatially interleaved neural populations in the insula and dorsomedial prefrontal cortex respond to unsigned RPE salience and valence-specific positive and negative RPEs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the same pattern of interleaved populations responding to valence-specific and unsigned RPE signals with identical emphasis and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Directional connectivity estimates suggest that the insula plays a leading role in communicating positive and unsigned reward prediction errors to the dorsomedial prefrontal cortex. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Directional connectivity estimates indicate that the insula plays a leading role in communicating positive and unsigned RPEs to the dorsomedial prefrontal cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same directional connectivity finding but with stronger certainty and framed as a result rather than medium-confidence conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings support asymmetric scaling across distinct but intermingled neural populations as a core principle in reward prediction error coding. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings support asymmetric coding across distinct but intermingled neural populations as a core principle of RPE processing. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core conclusion but slightly adjusts terminology (scaling -> coding); meaning is effectively the same and confidence remains high.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Local competition should favour the evolution of mating or foraging interference, and thus of reproductive skew. (certainty=high, type=hypothesis)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Local competition should favour the evolution of mating or foraging interference, and thus of reproductive skew. (certainty=high, type=hypothesis)
alignments.rationale: The hypothesis is stated identically in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Decreases in reproductive output caused by an individual’s accumulated fighting damage will favour less intense aggression but should have little influence on reproductive skew. (certainty=high, type=hypothesis)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Decreases in reproductive output caused by an individual's accumulated fighting damage will favour less intense aggression but should have little influence on reproductive skew. (certainty=high, type=hypothesis)
alignments.rationale: This hypothesis is preserved verbatim between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results from individual-based simulations support both hypotheses regarding social dominance and interference. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results from individual-based simulations support both hypotheses regarding social dominance and interference. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported simulation results are the same in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings can explain observed sex differences in reproductive skew and social dominance behaviour. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings can explain observed sex differences in reproductive skew and social dominance behaviour. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The explanatory conclusion is preserved unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Selectively inactivating OFC principal neurons when rats learned distinct cue-outcome associations disrupted subsequent model-based inference. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inactivating lOFC principal neurons during the learning of cue–outcome associations disrupts subsequent inference. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental manipulation and effect on subsequent inference are described equivalently in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: OFC inactivation led to generalized devaluation, which is explained by a deficit in defining credit assignment precision during model construction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: lOFC inactivation leads to generalized devaluation, which is inconsistent with a complete mapping failure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both note generalized devaluation after inactivation; the publication reframes the observation in relation to mapping failure, splitting observation and mechanism across claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The critical contribution of the OFC to learning is regulating the specificity of associations that comprise cognitive maps. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The lateral orbitofrontal cortex (lOFC) supports behavior by underlying map creation rather than by deploying cognitive maps to simulate outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's conclusion toward a role in map creation (rather than deployment); the core idea about OFC's role in map formation/specificity is preserved but confidence is reduced.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Head-fixed mice can be trained to play the iterative competitive game 'matching pennies' against a virtual computer opponent. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Head-fixed male mice can be trained to play the iterative competitive game 'matching pennies' against a virtual computer opponent. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication specifies 'male' mice whereas the preprint used the broader term 'mice', so the claim is clarified and slightly narrowed in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The animals’ performance is well described by a hybrid computational model that includes Q-learning and choice kernels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The animals' performance is well described by a hybrid computational model that includes Q-learning and choice kernels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The model description is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tasks encourage animals to operate at different regimes of reinforcement learning. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tasks encourage animals to operate at different regimes of reinforcement learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core claim about different RL regimes is kept; publication frames it more directly as a result but content is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pupil responses are modulated by observable variables, including choice and outcome, as well as latent variables for value updating, but not action selection. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pupil responses are modulated by observable variables, including choice and outcome, as well as latent variables for value updating, but not action selection. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The description of pupil-response modulation is identical across versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These results establish a paradigm for studying competitive decision-making in head-fixed mice and provide insights into the role of arousal-linked neuromodulation in the decision process. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study establishes a paradigm for studying competitive decision-making in head-fixed mice and provides insights into the role of arousal-linked neuromodulation in the decision process. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The contribution/claim is the same but the publication presents it with higher confidence and frames it as a concluding statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated high predictive power of cognitive and functional assessments (CFA) in predicting the severity of Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated high predictive power of cognitive and functional assessments (CFA) in predicting the severity of Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claim are textually identical and convey the same result and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive performance of models incorporating CFA was shown to be consistently higher in accuracy than those based solely on biomarker modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models incorporating CFA consistently showed higher accuracy than those based solely on biomarker modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative result with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimal SVM performance was observed for a set of four CFA test scores with multi-class classification accuracy of 83.0%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal SVM performance was observed for a set of four CFA test scores with multi-class classification accuracy of 83.0%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match of numeric performance and method between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performance of the KRR model was reported with combined CFA and MRI neuroimaging data, achieving R² = 0.874. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performance of the KRR model was reported with combined CFA and MRI neuroimaging data, achieving R² = 0.874. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about model, data fusion, and R² is identical in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We have developed an objective and practical computerized clinical decision support system (CDSS) to aid Alzheimer's disease diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We have developed an objective and practical computerized clinical decision support system (CDSS) to aid Alzheimer's disease diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is reproduced verbatim in the publication, with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Dataset selector (DS) feature in TPOT increases the system's efficiency by reducing computational expense through evaluating only a smaller subset of data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: FSS enhances TPOT's efficiency by allowing the selection of the best subset of features from smaller sets, thereby reducing computation time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication renames DS to FSS and frames the mechanism similarly (selecting a smaller subset to reduce compute), providing slightly clearer wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Template feature enforces type constraints and enables the incorporation of the Dataset selector at the beginning of each pipeline. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The introduction of Feature Set Selector (FSS) and Template in TPOT increases the system's scalability for big data applications. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication elevates the specific Template functionality to a higher-level contribution (scalability for big data), broadening the framing while retaining the Template's role.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TPOT-DS significantly outperforms a tuned XGBoost model and standard TPOT implementation in terms of computation time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TPOT-FSS significantly outperforms a tuned XGBoost model and standard TPOT implementation in simulations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts wording from a specific computational-time comparison to a broader statement of outperforming in simulations, changing the explicit metric reported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TPOT-DS corroborates that one of the modules is largely predictive of the clinical diagnosis of each individual in the context of major depressive disorder. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TPOT-FSS corroborates that one of the modules identified in a previous study is largely predictive of the clinical diagnosis of major depressive disorder. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report corroboration that a single module is largely predictive for diagnosis; the publication references prior identification but preserves the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimal signature discovered was a single 10-gene random forest multinomial signature that robustly discriminates active tuberculosis (TB) from other non-TB disease states with improved performance compared with previously published signatures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal signature discovered was a 10-gene random forest multinomial signature that robustly discriminated active tuberculosis from other non-TB disease states with an AUC of 0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe the same 10-gene signature, but the publication adds a specific AUC and omits the explicit comparative claim of improved performance versus prior signatures, altering the emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The signature specifically discriminates active TB/HIV co-infection from all other conditions with an AUC of 0.88. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 10-gene signature specifically discriminated active TB/HIV co-infection from all other conditions with an AUC of 0.88. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The AUC and the specific discrimination claim are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Examination of the expression patterns of signature genes suggests mechanisms underlying the unique inflammatory conditions associated with active TB in the presence of HIV. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Examination of the expression patterns of signature genes suggests mechanisms underlying the unique inflammatory conditions associated with active TB in the presence of HIV. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The exploratory/conclusion statement about mechanisms is reproduced verbatim and maintains the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Disregulation of CD8+ effector T-cell and NK-cell associated genes may be an important feature of Mtb/HIV co-infection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dysregulation of CD8+ effector T-cell and NK-cell associated genes may be an important feature of Mtb/HIV co-infection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about dysregulation of CD8+ and NK-cell genes is preserved (minor spelling difference only), with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hydrophobic gates in ion channels result in local de-wetting of pores which functionally closes them to water and ion permeation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined influence of radius and hydrophobicity on pore dewetting has been quantified using molecular dynamics simulations and machine learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
